videos.title: Super fast OSL
videos.description:
A two-minute airbrush OSL pass on yesterday's model. Honestly I think OSL usually looks silly on the tabletop, but I want to be able to say I don't like it because I think it's dumb, not because I can't do it.

What's in this one:
- Warlord Purple base layer through the airbrush, masked with an offcut of paper so I don't spray my knee
- Squid Pink as the core glow
- Watered-down white blended wet-on-wet to pick up the pink and finish the glow
- Tabletop-level OSL in about two minutes
- First finished model of 2026

videos.title: Kal Arath Live Play Episode 3
videos.description:
Looting the cultists, getting attacked by six wolves at night, and using Manalone's Twin Sun Sutra cult generator to find out exactly which sect we just pissed off.

What happens this session:
- Loot rolls: a carved ivory idol and an enchanted amulet
- Reading the desecrated giant skull — ancient, strange, flourishing
- A brass lantern inside the skull = a clue about who tried to burn it
- Three story threads now: the cult, the desecrators, and the original battle barge crash
- Camping in the forest — six wolves attack at night, Garen goes unconscious, mantis Freddy saves the day
- Self-vs-self oracle roll for the mantis fighting his solitary nature, and rolling a six (yes — he stays)
- Generating the cult with the Twin Sun Sutra supplement: The Transcendent Order of the Hidden Mysteries, headquartered in the Hidden Iron Flower Fortress
- Building the mantis as a real PC — Pact of Blood magic user with the draining spell

Plus a level-up walkthrough for Garen and how the rolling-against-yourself mechanic in Kal Arath works when you have an NPC ally.

videos.title: Kal Arath Episode 2
videos.description:
Putting Manalone's Prima Materia oracle dice to work in a solo Kal Arath session. Garen Vosque and the giant praying mantis bond, get caught in a storm, and stumble onto a desecrated giant skull cult shrine.

In this session:
- Using Prima Materia dice to interpret motives and relationships instead of yes/no rolls
- Reading dice casts as Jungian archetypes (Fortune above Mind, self vs self, Knox shadow principle)
- Hex weather rolls force a major storm and a cave shelter
- Investigating the giant skull and finding it ancient, strange, and flourishing
- A Knox die "peek" reveals an entanglement with the cult is inevitable
- Drawing the cave map quick-and-dirty Runehammer style
- Combat: mantis combat-leaps and bites, two cultists down

Why I'm using the Prima Materia dice instead of just the Kal Arath tables: Kal Arath's tables are excellent. The dice keep you in the mystical mode—you're interpreting, not table-flipping. Both have their place.

videos.title: Tips for batch painting unique models
videos.description:
How to batch-paint a commission where every model is different. The trick: group by paint and material, not by armor panel.

The methodology:
- Most batch-painting advice assumes identical models — same robe, same shoulder pad, same boot
- For unique commission squads, instead think about **what's on your brush right now**
- Group every surface that takes the same paint or technique, regardless of which body part or model
- All NMM in one pass across every model that has any metal — armor, blade, buckle, doesn't matter
- Then all airbrush blues and oranges in one pass
- Then all leather, all faces, all flesh — by material, not by model

Walking through how I'm staging the Infinity commission this way: every model prepped for the next round of NMM, even though some are doing armor panels and some are doing shirts that the box art renders as NMM.

Plus a teaser of the Manalone Prima Materia dice — high-quality stone alchemical-symbol oracle dice. Full review video coming once I really know the system.

videos.title: No making the same mistakes as I usually do
videos.description:
Found two Death Watch upgrade sprues and an Imperial Agents Combat Patrol buried in the pile. Time to lay down some ground rules for coming back to 40k for 11th edition without falling into the same FOMO traps as last time.

The rules (for me — and maybe for you too):

1. **F the rules.** Build a tournament-legal 2,000-point Imperial Agents army with no Legends, but don't care about winning, the meta, or being competitive. Build the third-edition Daemonhunters army I drooled over as a kid.

2. **No metawatching.** No Goonhammer, no subreddit, no Discord competitive talk. The whole edition. Instagram and YouTube only for hobby/inspiration. The FOMO loop ends here.

3. **No pre-orders.** Used only when I do need additions. I don't care if some hotness drops—I'll get it eventually.

4. **Probably just: have fun hobbying again.**

A modern take on the third-edition Daemonhunters — entirely converted, entirely kitbashed. Maybe never sees a real game and that's fine. Just need to plug back into Warhammer and out of the commercial loop.

videos.description:
Career changes pulled all my game days. Pillage and Midgard are officially shelved, and the dream of getting any Warmachine games in for the foreseeable future is dead.

Letting it go and reorienting:
- Pillage Normans and Welsh — 99% painted but never going to play them. Will finish and shelf them.

It's mixed. It's been a fight since I started this channel to figure out how to actually play. Now I have the last nail in the coffin: there's no hope of real games. So a couple of years to really focus on the brush.

After the Infinity commission and Dusk commission wrap (about a month of painting), I'll reorient and figure out where the channel goes next.

videos.title: Painting some infinity models
videos.description:
Crunch week on the Infinity commission. Building, priming, and base-coating the whole force in a single day — airbrushing zenithal under the alien centerpiece, then a hard pivot to contrast paints over zenithal for the troopers because it's faster than swapping the airbrush over.

What's covered:
- Why Siocast (or whatever Corvus Belli is using now) is awful to assemble
- Airbrushing greens up to a sickly yellow belly on the alien centerpiece — no tutorials, just vibing
- The scary white pass right in the middle of his face
- Why I'm putting the airbrush down for the troopers and going contrast over zenithal
- Speed paint tips: synthetic brush (longer the better), shake them, no wet palette, just a couple drops in the well
- Building Tenebris up with gray for the black armor highlights
- Five hours in, finally feeling like I might hit the deadline

The sink fell off the wall and took the water line with it earlier this week, so cleaning the airbrush means going upstairs—a real time cost when you can just brush the base coats instead.

videos.title: What's a Rectifier? Fixing My Grandfather's Dead Heat Gun
videos.description:
Taking apart my grandfather's heat gun to figure out what blew. Turns out that little black component I assumed was a capacitor is actually a rectifier—the thing that converts AC wall current to DC for the motor and handles the hot/cold/off switching.

Not a painting night. Just a small electronics autopsy. Next step is tracking one down and soldering it back in to save this thing.

Just trying to survive. Rest in peace to the heat gun. Hobby or die.

videos.title: Kal Arath Live Play Session 1
videos.description:
A full solo RPG session in Kal Arath, live and unedited. Rolling up Garen Vosque—a decommissioned soldier abandoned in the desert with a bag of gold, 2 HP, and a vague sense of direction—and stumbling out of the desert into an encounter with a profane giant praying mantis carrying a piece of a wrecked battle barge.

Character creation, equipment rolls, two days of hex travel, weather and foraging turns, and a first real encounter driven by Oracle rolls and reaction checks. Also: drawing my own hex map with a watercolor brush pen that turned out to be very much not waterproof, tinting the entire desert red.

The whole point of this kit and this system is removing the barriers—no miniatures, no big terrain setups, just a notebook, some dice, and whatever story comes out. Solo RPG as a creative and personal development exercise, not a quest for perfection.

videos.description:
Painting non-metallic blue steel scales using Scale Color Artist paints while having a late-night breakdown about the hobby, commissions, and what I'm even doing with my life at 215 days into the daily vlog.

The technique: starting with Tenebris Gray from AK as a bluish black base, building up with Scale Color Artist violet gray, edge highlighting with pure white, then glazing Vallejo Game Air electric blue over everything for the blue steel tint. Real talk about hobby burnout, the Maximus model that's been kicking my ass for months, missing Pillage nights, and why I'm afraid to stop the daily vlogs even when I want to pivot to higher production value content. Also: sitting down to finish commissions, then rethinking everything.

videos.title: Using speed paints as a wash
videos.description:
Continuing the red wash experiments—mixing Slaughter Red speed paint with Army Painter Quick Shade medium gives an even better detail pickout than the brown wash, without killing any vibrance.

Side-by-side comparison with yesterday's brown wash—the speed paint mix pulls detail out of the deeper cracks and actually makes the red look MORE vibrant, not less. Ice King suggested trying this and it's definitely the way to go for the Dusk army going forward.

videos.title: How to get better by playing yourself
videos.description:
A complete guide to solo Warmachine practice—how to learn a new army in days instead of months by playing against yourself.

The key principles:
1. **Pick a game with no hidden information.** Warmachine is perfect because all abilities, strategies, and lists are public knowledge. This would never work with something like Infinity.
2. **Terrain and table skew.** Give yourself the bad side of the table, the opponent the good side. The hardest part of solo play is making sure you're not accidentally giving yourself an advantage.
3. **Play short games.** Just the first 2-3 turns, then pick it back up and start over. This maximizes deployment practice—where I lose most of my real games.
4. **Don't dunk on yourself.** The goal isn't to win. Play the opposing side as hard as possible to learn the army you're actually trying to master.
5. **Takebacks encouraged.** Be strict with yourself, lenient with your opponent. Fix their mistakes so it's harder for you.

Specific lessons from my Infernals vs Menoth learning game:
- Threat ranges and charge lanes
- How "tough ignore" units mulch infantry
- Realistic spell economy for my assassination caster (2-3 casts, not 4)
- New Steamroller scenario layouts

This saves months of getting kicked in real games. I can run through 3-4 short games in a day and learn what would take weeks of waiting for real opponents. WarTable is great, but I don't always have time for full matches.

TIMESTAMPS
0:00 Why I played myself
1:30 Step 1: Pick the right game system
4:00 Step 2: Deployment and table setup
7:00 Step 3: Play short games
9:30 Step 4: Don't dunk on yourself

videos.title: How to paint white robes
videos.description:
Quick walkthrough on painting white cloth robes over a purple and red base coat using buff and vanilla white from Scale 75. This is a fast, infantry-friendly approach—maybe 10 minutes total on the robe.

The process: buff base layer over the purple/red undercoat, then selective glazes of vanilla white on the raised edges, painting in the direction of the fabric flow to build intentional texture. Finishing with pure white edge highlights on the sharpest folds. Keeping the tinted buff in the deepest shadows gives it depth without spending hours blending.

This is a test model for an Orcs & Goblins army—these guys are frontline troops, so the goal is looking good on the table without Golden Daemon effort.

videos.title: How to start commission painting and why you maybe shouldn’t.
videos.description:
Real talk on commission painting—how to get started, how to price your work, and why turning your hobby into a business might not be what you expect.

Three ways to find clients:
1. Show up to your local game store with painted models. Someone will ask you to paint theirs.
2. Post your work on social media with "commissions open" in your bio. People will come to you.
3. Paint models and sell them on eBay (harder than it sounds—painted models usually lose value unless you're painting at a very high level in popular faction colors).

For pricing, go to flgstudio.com and look at Frontline Gaming's commission pricing spreadsheet. Don't reinvent the wheel—use their rates as your baseline.

The honest part: anytime you turn something you love into something you do for money, it risks sucking the joy out of it. Everything becomes a deadline. Everything becomes "is this good enough?" I've been through this cycle multiple times with different businesses. Sometimes it's better to keep the day job and keep painting as the thing that fills your bucket back up.

#commissionpainting #miniaturepainting #hobbyadvice #hobbyvlog

videos.title: Making zines is easy with the right tools.
videos.description:
Making zines and hand-bound books is way easier than you think with the right tools! Tonight I'm printing and assembling five Castle Grief / Calarrath RPG booklets, plus Coptic binding a poetry book for my wife.

I walk through the whole process: scoring pages with a scoring board (the single most important tool), folding, and saddle stitching with a Bostitch stapler. I used to make and sell zines and notebooks pre-COVID—when you're cranking out a hundred a day, these tools are non-negotiable.

Tips covered:
• Why scoring makes folding 10x easier and prevents tearing
• The finger-spacing method for staple placement (no ruler needed)
• How to bust through five zines in 30 minutes including print time
• Coptic binding with waxed archival thread from Maine Thread Company
• Using a purpose-driven rock with a notch carved in it (yes, really)

I also hand-bind a copy of my wife's poetry book that we forgot to keep for ourselves during the original run with hand-marbled, dyed covers.

videos.description:
More paint on the Norman test model! Tonight I'm working through leather, chainmail, gold buckles, blue fabric, and skin tones—trying to get this guy close to finished.

Gold on all the buckles, then I try a deep turquoise blue on the fabric peeking out from under the chainmail and it actually looks great as a one-pass speed paint effect over the zenithal.

In this video I sit down to build Sunrot, Miscast, and Gardens of Hecate miniatures while talking about why building is the most relaxing part of the hobby for me. A couple hours disappear and you come out the other side with built models and a clear head. That's the whole point.

videos.title: How to paint leather with just two colors
videos.description:
Paint convincing worn leather in minutes with just two colors! In this quick tutorial, I race the clock against NyQuil to paint a leather belt on one of my Norman miniatures using Satchel Brown and Serpent Bite Leather.

My usual leather recipe (Burnt Umber, M Frame Brown, and XV-88) was dead in the pot, so I improvised a dead-simple two-color method. Satchel Brown goes down as a dark reddish-brown base, then Serpent Bite Leather gets stippled on top for highlights. The key is thinking about how real leather works—anywhere it bends or flexes, you get pullup, that lighter color showing through at the edges.

I walk through the stippling technique, show how to blend out mistakes, and talk about why stippling sells the worn leather look better than edge highlighting or glazing for quick tabletop results. If you're painting an army and don't want to spend forever on every belt and pouch, this is the way.

videos.title: KDM Episode 8: Screaming Antelope Hunt Phase
videos.description:
Building the Screaming Antelope and completing the hunt phase before our first antelope showdown! Also: the dogs got into the game room and chaos ensued.

The dogs can apparently open the door handle now. Lost all the dice (cat was probably batting them around), had to clean up a disaster zone. Lesson learned: lock the door.

The Screaming Antelope was a fun build - did some on the couch while recovering. Little sanding on the head, some gap filling, and it's drying. Might need to pin that wonky pose.

Hunt phase highlights:
- Teething event: Sophia and Alexios both roll 10s! Two large antelope resources before the fight even starts
- Kip takes arm damage with no armor there (risky for our archer)
- Grim and Frostbitten drains some survival
- The Beginning event gives everyone +1 survival back

Ready to face the Screaming Antelope tomorrow. First new monster type!

#kingdomdeath #screamingantelope #sologaming

videos.title: I took two days to paint a candy cane.
videos.description:
Keeping the hobby streak alive post-vasectomy! If I can hobby through RSV, bronchitis, pneumonia, and an ER trip, I can do it through this.

Quick update tonight - finished the candy cane on my Butcher model. It kicked my ass between the recovery pain and coffee shakes, but two days later it's done. Not perfect, but good enough for tabletop.

Lego tip: Check out Whatnot for bulk Lego deals! Retail stores use it to liquidate inventory - I got hundreds of pieces for 2-3 bucks. Just avoid the trading cards (sketchy stuff there). Stick to physical products.

Also getting close to having my D&D Lego system fully fleshed out. Waiting on some bulk orders before I can show off the layouts and bases. The game dev journey continues!

Paint on model. Streak alive. See you tomorrow.

---
🌐 Website & Blog: https://hobbynomicon.com
---

#hobbystreak #miniaturepainting #lego

videos.title: Keeping the hobby streak alive.
videos.description:
I got a vasectomy today and I'm still painting. The hobby streak continues!

Look - I hobbied through RSV, bronchitis, pneumonia, and an emergency room trip for oxygen. I didn't break the streak then. I'm not breaking it now.

Tonight I'm painting the candy cane on my Butcher model. Yes, I'm in pain. Yes, my hand is shaking. But paint is going on a model.

The actual operation was super fast - didn't feel a thing. But 9 hours later? It sucks. This is going to be a quick one.

I've put paint on a model every day this year. Even if it's just a single coat, the streak continues. If I can do it during all those other health disasters, I can do it today.

See you tomorrow.

---
🌐 Website & Blog: https://hobbynomicon.com
---

#hobbystreak #motivation #miniaturepainting

videos.title: KDM Episode 7
videos.description:
Deep settlement phase management in Kingdom Death Monster - crafting gear, weapon proficiency decisions, and preparing for the Screaming Antelope!

This episode is all about the settlement phase strategy. Built two Lion Guitars, King Spear, and White Lion Coat. The perfect organ gamble paid off huge - our new survivor rolled through multiple age milestones and now crits on 8s with nothing equipped.

But here's the catch: he doesn't have enough hunts left to master a weapon proficiency. He'll be our "ringer" - if the main archer dies, this guy pops in with the death mask critting on 4+.

Key decisions this session: Clinestra becomes matchmaker (rotating in/out for intimacy events), Sophia gets the pounce combo with her new cloak, and we're up to 8 population.

Real talk: this settlement phase took 1.5 hours. Set aside 2-3 hours if you want to get through a full hunt session.

#kingdomdeath #settlementphase #sologaming

videos.title: International Lego day and more KDM - Episode 6
videos.description:
International Lego Day mail haul plus the best Kingdom Death Monster hunt yet! Starting my Lego D&D game development journey.

I've accepted it - competitive wargaming outside the house isn't happening for a while. So I'm channeling that energy into developing a simple D&D system my 4 and 6-year-old can play using Legos. Got some amazing Ninjago minifigures for $20 and hit up Bricks and Mini Figs for rats and spiders - perfect starter encounter for $10.

videos.title: First One Piece Event Prep.

Tomorrow is a One Piece pre-release event—the perfect entry point for any new card game. Sealed format means you buy packs and play with what you open, so no deck building or collection needed. I used to play Magic professionally (made more money than my day job back then), and card games have been a void in my life since Hasbro destroyed Magic.

My sealed strategy that works across all TCGs: prioritize big beefy creatures on curve. In limited formats, removal is scarce, so just flood the board with threats. Aim to play something every turn, using all your resources each turn (curving out). Look for cards you can discard to pump your creatures—One Piece's counter system rewards card advantage.

Event safety tip from my rough area comic shop days: keep belongings in a backpack, foot through the strap when sitting. Only bring what you need—I'm bringing just a small binder and deck box. Don't flash valuable cards.

Also finished the Going Merry Lego set—hands down the most fun Lego build I've ever done, including all my childhood sets. Highly recommend for any One Piece fan!

videos.title: Bald man rants about AI
videos.description:
Honest thoughts on generative AI and why creative work should stay human. While painting Warmachine Toro warjacks, I share my concerns about AI replacing artistic expression—not the copyright debates, but something deeper.

The creative act—drawing, painting, writing—is what makes us human. It's the manifestation of that divine spark, whatever you believe gives it to us. When generative AI replaces the process of creating art, we lose something fundamental about being human. I use AI for productivity (emails, coding help, work tasks), but I won't use it to create anything meaningful.

What happens when kids grow up generating images instead of learning to draw? When they never feel called to pick up a paintbrush or a pen? The entry-level creative jobs are already disappearing—advertising, animation, commercial art—and those were pathways for people to make a living being creative.

I'm not saying don't use AI image generators if that's your thing. But consider: does prompting an AI feel the same as making something yourself? For me, it felt like using a cheat code in a video game—briefly exciting, then hollow.

My take: use AI to handle the inhuman work, the productivity stuff, the BS that eats up your day. Then use that freed-up time to create with your hands. Don't let the tools that could give us more time for creativity instead replace creativity entirely.

videos.title: Quick base work.
videos.description:
Industrial base painting with weathering powders and liquid chrome! Quick techniques for metal panel bases using dry brushing, rust effects, and metallic scratches.

Day 113 and tackling the base on our Crucible Guard model. Industrial metal panel bases need special treatment—here's the quick method that works.

The technique: white dry brush on the dirt areas between panels to create brightness, then silver dry brush to pick up metallic flakes on the high points. Brownish Decay speed paint over the white creates an oily, dirty ground effect that reads as workshop floor.

Weathering powders are the secret weapon: Tamiya rust orange in the middle of panels (edges stay worn and silver), creating realistic oxidation patterns. The orange complements the teal armor beautifully—complementary colors always work.

Final touch: Vallejo Liquid Chrome on bolt heads and sharp panel edges. Just tiny dots that catch light and add definition. Don't overdo it—less is more with chrome effects.

The grass tufts got some decay treatment too for that overgrown industrial look. Still not 100% satisfied with these bases, but they're significantly better than before the weathering pass. Sometimes you just have to keep iterating.

The most important lesson? Don't let perfect be the enemy of good. From tabletop distance, this Toro looks amazing despite all the issues I'm critical of up close. That's what matters for gaming miniatures.

videos.title: How to paint crucible guard green.
videos.description:
Crucible Guard green cheat code! Three speed paints through an airbrush create that perfect faction green with smooth transitions—Thunderbird Blue, Plasmatic Bolt, and Tyrant Navy.

The secret: airbrush these speed paints working BACKWARDS from highlight to shadow. Start with Thunderbird Blue on the highest points over a white zenithal, then build shadows on top. Speed paints are transparent, so spraying dark over light works better than the reverse.

Step by step: Thunderbird Blue on upper surfaces and edges (with a strong zenithal, you might get away with just this one color). Plasmatic Bolt builds the midtone shadows from below. Tyrant Navy punches the deepest shadows—go slow and careful here since the contrast is intense.

The nerve-wracking part: messing up the dark means ruining the lighter areas underneath. But that's why you go slow. These speed paints dry smoother than they appear wet, and you're saving massive time compared to traditional layering.

Fix for speckling: if your airbrush clogs and speckles dark on light areas, go back to Thunderbird Blue with a touch of white ink and blast over the mistake. Tomorrow: cutting in details and hopefully finishing these warjacks!

videos.title: A Christmas oil wash.
videos.description:
Quick Christmas Day hobby session applying an oil wash to my terrain board! Using burnt umber oil paint thinned with mineral spirits, I show how to add depth and richness to drybrushed terrain.

Oil washes are incredible for terrain because they settle into all the cracks and crevices while being easy to manipulate before they dry. This transforms the bright, Mars-like drybrushing from the previous session back into natural earth and dirt tones. The contrast between the drybrushed highlights and the dark recesses really brings terrain to life.

videos.title: Painting dirt. Hobby vlog 99
videos.description:
Painting realistic dirt and earth on wargaming terrain! Day 99 hobby vlog covering wet blending craft paints for natural ground effects on game boards.

Day 99 of the hobby streak and we're painting dirt. The terrain board is sealed and ready for color—time to turn gray primer into realistic earth using cheap craft paints and wet blending techniques.

The method: Apple Barrel Nutmeg brown as the midtone base, bronze flesh tone (warm orange) dotted on highlights, transparent burnt umber ink in the shadows. Mix everything directly on the board surface with a big makeup brush, blending while wet.

Key techniques: use a gloved hand and paper towel to sponge excess paint, creating natural texture variation. Butcher paper adds additional texture through the absorption pattern. Work in circles, getting smaller each pass to ensure even coverage.

It looks like wet mud while working—that's correct. When dry, it reads as realistic earth. The color variation from the wet blend creates natural-looking ground without tedious layering.

Also opened Mage Knight Dungeons boosters (Christmas present to myself) and unboxed a limited edition book from 9th Circle Press. Day 100 tomorrow will finish the board with oil wash, dry brushing, and hopefully wrapping up all the building projects to get back to pure painting.

videos.title: How to make scratch built terrain indestructible
videos.description:
How to make scratch-built foam terrain indestructible! The secret sealing technique that lets your terrain survive years of gaming abuse.

Day 98 and pushing through despite being sick. The technique: multiple layers of diluted Elmer's glue (50% water, 50% glue, touch of isopropyl alcohol), then a final coat of Modge Podge matte mixed with primer. This creates a rock-hard shell over foam without losing detail.

Key tip: thin your Modge Podge with water to avoid filling in texture details. If you go too thick, dab with paper towel immediately to remove excess. Make sure to seal any exposed pink foam edges so spray paint won't melt them.

This is how I built all my Warhammer Fantasy gaming tables back in the day—hills that could take real abuse. After 4+ layers of the glue mixture plus the Modge Podge seal, this terrain board will be completely locked in and ready for rattle cans, airbrushing, and years of gaming.

The magician class gets 1d4 HP, a spell book (rolled Treaties on Force and Dissolution with Crystal Resonance, Floating Disc, and Vapors of Dream), and starts with basic equipment plus adventuring gear rolled on the d20 table. Our guy ended up with torches, oil flask, sledgehammer, and chalk.

Key rules I clarify: moon signs affect your character based on birth phase (waxing or waning), skills default to 6-in-6, and the book includes everything you need including weapon stats and spell descriptions. The spells are flavorful—Vapors of Dream puts creatures to sleep and a bladed weapon kills them instantly while enchanted.

All characters speak Waldish plus additional languages based on Intelligence modifier. Ready to play Dolmenwood with fully completed character sheets!

videos.description:
Dolmenwood Players Book flip through and review! Exploring the character creation options, races, and classes in this OSR fantasy setting from Necrotic Gnome. Perfect for anyone considering running or playing Dolmenwood.

In this video I walk through the Players Book from the Dolmenwood Kickstarter, covering all the playable races including the elf-like Woodgrues (my personal favorite), the antler-headed Mosslings, and classic humans. I discuss how the classes work in this old-school revival system—you have your traditional fighters, magic users, and thieves, but with unique Dolmenwood flavor like the Friar class.

videos.title: Painting the wood on The Love Shack.
videos.description:
Weathered wood painting tutorial using Marco Frisone's recipe! Dry brushing and contrast paint technique for old wood terrain on the Love Shack objective.

Speed painting weathered wood on the Love Shack objective for the Warmachine Winter Wonderland painting competition. Following Marco Frisone's recipe: Vallejo Vanilla White dry brush, then Plague Bearer Flesh, Guilliman Flesh, and Militarum Green contrast paints.

The technique: dry brush the vanilla white from your light source direction (I used a big makeup brush for smoother texture). Heat gun everything dry before contrast paints to avoid goopy messes. Apply Plague Bearer Flesh all over, then Guilliman Flesh more selectively in shadow areas, and finally Militarum Green very sparingly in the deepest recesses.

Working wet creates natural blending between the contrast paints—almost wet blending them together. The key is not overworking areas and accepting some happy accidents. Where dry brushing gets pulled up, it actually adds to the worn character.

Pro tip: dry brush up and down to simulate wood grain. Use the lamp as your light source guide. Let layers dry between coats to avoid stripping previous work.

Results: high-contrast weathered wood that reads well on the table. Perfect for objectives and terrain where speed matters more than perfection. Snow effects and metals tomorrow to finish before the competition deadline.

videos.title: How to marble paper.
videos.description:
Paper marbling tutorial for book covers! Learn the traditional technique using carrageenan, ink, and Kodak Photoflow to create stunning marbled paper for DIY bookbinding projects.

Day 81 of vlogs and we're making 100 marbled covers for my wife's poetry book. The setup: carrageenan (seaweed-based gelling agent) dissolved in water creates surface tension that holds ink in place. Kodak Professional Photoflow breaks that tension in controlled ways, creating those beautiful cell patterns.

The technique: drop ink onto the carrageenan water surface, watch it float and spread. Use brushes, homemade broom-twine whisks, or your fingers to create patterns. Blow on it, drag strings through it, or just let the natural cells form. Then lay your paper on top, lift quickly to avoid smearing, and hang to dry.

Pro tips: let carrageenan fully dissolve (rushing = failed marble). Quick paper lifts prevent drag marks. Traditional bookbinders use nail boards to create systematic patterns. I prefer the organic bubble look.

Also showing tabard painting progress on my competition mini—three hours in, learning that fabric painting needs work. Identifying weaknesses is how you improve. Sometimes the most technically "hobby" days involve completely different art forms!

videos.title: How I clean my resin 3d prints.
videos.description:
How to clean resin 3D prints properly for miniatures! My complete workflow for getting matte, clean prints without that glossy uncured resin look.

The secret to clean 3D prints is patience and multiple washing stages. I let prints drain on the build plate first so loose resin runs off before they even touch the wash station. Then I use denatured ethanol (check your resin's MSDS—different resins recommend different solvents).

My process: wash in the Elegoo station, but pre-dip in a secondary container of old ethanol first to extend the life of my main bath. After the alcohol wash, I remove supports in hot water, then rinse under running water to remove all alcohol residue. The key step everyone skips: let prints air dry completely overnight before curing. This lets all the alcohol and water evaporate.

The difference shows in the finish—properly cleaned prints look matte with only the layer lines catching light. Glossy prints mean there was still liquid resin on the surface when cured. The area under hollow prints often stays glossy if resin was trapped inside.

I also filter and reuse my ethanol with a cone filter, cure all supports and waste resin in the sun before disposal, and run dirty water into a curing bucket instead of down the drain. Proper resin handling takes longer but the results are worth it.

videos.title: Painting an NMM Belt Buckle With Scale 75 Grays
videos.description:
NMM belt buckle painting breakdown! Volumetric highlights and non-metallic metal techniques explained while painting a tiny detail on an orcs and goblins model.

Sometimes the smallest details teach the biggest lessons. This video focuses entirely on painting one belt buckle using non-metallic metal techniques—and the volumetric thinking that makes NMM work.

The core concept: forget that you're painting metal. Instead, think about where light hits a reflective surface and paint those bright spots directly. The buckle is just a small rectangle, but getting the highlight placement right sells the entire illusion. Light source consistency matters more than blending perfection.

Volumetric painting means understanding your subject as a 3D form and placing lights and shadows based on actual physics, not just edge highlighting everything. A belt buckle curves, has depth, catches light differently on its face versus its edges. Paint what light actually does, not what you think metal "should" look like.

This micro-tutorial approach—extreme focus on one tiny element—often teaches more than trying to explain an entire model at once. Master the buckle, apply the same thinking to swords, armor plates, and everything else metallic. NMM is a skill that transfers once you understand the underlying principles.

videos.title: Painting a leather belt.
videos.description:
High-quality leather belt painting tutorial following Richard Gray's method! Texture building with brush strokes, Bugman's Glow highlights, and worn leather effects.

Day one of taking painting seriously. Following Richard Gray's leather belt guide to paint display-quality leather on a miniature. Fresh Winsor Newton Series 7 brush finally getting used after sitting in the package for years.

The recipe: base coat dark brown, build up with Mournfang Brown creating texture through deliberate brush strokes. Add Bugman's Glow very sparingly on wear points—where leather bends, edges, and high-contact areas. The pink undertone reads as worn flesh-colored leather, not actual pink.

The technique: this isn't stippling or edge highlighting. It's sketching texture with careful brush strokes, building up layers that create a cracked, well-used leather effect. Let each layer dry completely to avoid tearing previous work—that's the secret to smooth finishes.

Final highlights with buff/bone color only on the most extreme points. Then Dark Violet wash glazed into recesses to reinforce shadows without overwhelming the work.

Results after 90 minutes: leather that looks genuinely worn and textured. The camera doesn't capture it well, but in person the effect is striking. This level of detail seemed impossible a year ago—turns out it's just patience, brush control, and deciding to actually try.

Starting a solo Crown and Skull campaign and explaining why this system works brilliantly for playing alone. All rolls are player-facing, monster AI is built into the tactics system, and each hex has random generation tables that eliminate prep work entirely.

The Runehammer quality: these books are artifacts. Leather bound, gold foil, thick fibrous pages that feel amazing. The dice are chunky boys that thunk when rolled. This is passion project craftsmanship from someone who cares about making good products.

The dice philosophy: only using Newtonian solids (platonic shapes)—no d10s, no d4s. Original D&D didn't have them, so neither will I. D8 halved for d4 results, d20 twice for percentiles. It's extra work but adds esoteric flavor.

The gameplay: roll d10 for location when entering a hex (elven ruins, crumbled temples, forest clearings), then roll again for events (monster encounters, NPC meetings, treasure finds). Zero prep required—the system generates content on the fly.

Next up: generating characters using the official builder and my own esoteric methods. Dolmenwood campaign incoming.

Hot take: resin 3D printing might not be worth the time investment. After printing almost daily for 2-3 years and producing over a thousand miniatures, I share my honest thoughts on whether owning a resin printer makes sense for hobbyists.

When it works, 3D printing is fantastic—dialed-in settings, known resins, and repeatable files produce great results. But the moment you introduce any variability, things go wrong fast. Hobby pressure and the pile of shame spiral—why too many projects kills your joy and how to break the cycle through discipline and purging.

Real talk about hobby pressure while painting goblins in ultimate goblin mode (hunched over, extreme back pain, strangely comfortable). The trap we all fall into: using small time windows to build "just one guy" until suddenly you have three unpainted armies.

The cycle: 5 minutes free? Build something. Hour to paint? Prime what you just built. Repeat for five years. Now you're sitting in front of a mountain of half-finished projects, paralyzed by choice, spending your precious hobby time just thinking about what to paint.

The solution: discipline and purging. Sell everything you won't realistically paint this year. Sell anything you could easily rebuy later. Keep sentimental pieces and finished work. Go into next year with a clean slate and zero hobby pressure.

The deeper point: for lifestyle hobbyists, this isn't just a pastime—it's spiritual practice, creative expression, the thing that keeps you sane. Until your pile of shame owns you and the thing that should bring joy becomes stress.

Next year's plan: one Warmachine faction, focus on quality over quantity, maybe only 10-20 display-level models. No more chasing whims. Time to lock in and get actually good at this.

videos.title: My tips on Buying / Selling / Trading Warhammer Miniatures
videos.description:
How to buy, sell, and trade Warhammer miniatures safely! eBay tips, Reddit miniswap guide, and why you should ONLY use PayPal Goods and Services for protection.

Drowning in unpainted plastic? Time for a purge. Here's how to safely buy, sell, and trade miniatures without getting scammed—North America focused, but principles apply everywhere.

The platforms that work: eBay (easy listing, buyer protection built in), Reddit r/miniswap (great deals on abandoned projects), game-specific Discord servers and Facebook groups (community deals, but verify sellers). Raw Facebook Marketplace is scalper territory—stick to dedicated hobby groups.

The golden rule: ONLY use PayPal Goods and Services. Period. If someone asks for Friends and Family or any other payment method, they're scamming you. That "too good to be true" deal? It is. Protect yourself.

Trading safely: both parties PayPal G&S each other the agreed value. Yes, you both pay fees—consider it insurance. Never ship first hoping they'll ship back. I've seen too many people send armies and receive boxes of rocks.

eBay seller tips: include shipping in your price, offer free shipping, ship Priority Mail so you don't need to buy boxes. Accept offers with a minimum threshold set. Photos don't need to be professional—just make sure ONLY the item you're selling is in frame to avoid disputes.

Use the hobby to fund the hobby. Sell what you won't paint to buy what you will.

videos.title: The Jankiest Rust Tutorial Ever.
videos.description:
Three rust painting methods compared! Dirty Down Rust, AK Interactive Rust Streaks, and Secret Weapon pigments tested on goblin spear tips—honest results.

Late night rust tutorial because requests demanded it. Testing three methods on goblin spears: the $20 Dirty Down Rust bottle, AK Interactive Rust Streaks enamel, and the old Secret Weapon orange pigment stipple method.

Dirty Down Rust: stipple it on thick, let it dry looking terrible, then reactivate with water to push it around. Edge highlight silver back with a dry brush at the end. When done right, it looks insanely realistic—almost too good, like cartoon metal meeting real rust. But it's finicky, expensive for the tiny bottle, and benefits massively from Typhus Corrosion underneath for texture.

AK Rust Streaks: works like Streaking Grime. More versatile—use as wash, filter, or standard enamel. Better for tanks and rivets than small weapons. Clean up with mineral spirits.

Old school method: brown oil wash, stipple bright orange with torn foam, dry brush silver edges. Faster, more forgiving, nearly as good at tabletop distance.

The honest verdict: Dirty Down looks best when perfected but takes the most work. For 90 goblin spears, the old foam stipple method wins on efficiency.

videos.title: I fixed my contrast paint problems.
videos.description:
I fixed my contrast/speed paint problems! The secret: use synthetic brushes, not natural hair. Shout out to commenter Jay for this game-changing tip.

All the robes are done and I'm zooming through the remaining goblins in regular clothes. What changed? Switched from sable to synthetic brushes. The difference is massive.

Why synthetic works better with speed paints:
- No wicking up into the ferrule
- Paint doesn't dry as quickly on the brush
- Long-bristle synthetics hold more paint
- Quick water rinse actually cleans them (every 3 models)

I was fighting my tools this whole time. The paint wasn't the problem—Speed Paint 2.0 works fine—it was the brush wicking paint away before I could apply it properly. Now 10 models take 15 minutes instead of being a struggle.

Lesson learned: sometimes contrast paints don't suck, you're just using the wrong brush. If you've been frustrated with speed paints or Citadel Contrast pulling up or drying weird, try a synthetic with a long well before you give up on them entirely.

videos.title: How to paint pale orc flesh.
videos.description:
No-nonsense guide to painting grimdark pale orc flesh! Pallid Witch Flesh base, Streaking Grime magic, and the UHU glue spit trick for disgusting details.

The recipe:
1. Base coat everything in Pallid Witch Flesh
2. Mix Quick Shade Wash Medium with Bugman's Glow 50/50 for mouths, hands, elbows—anywhere flesh is pinker
3. Cover the entire model with Streaking Grime
4. Wipe away with makeup sponge and Q-tip—this is what turns the skin green
5. Use mineral spirits on muscle tops, teeth, and nails for extra contrast
6. Highlight with Menoth White on nails, teeth, and muscle peaks
7. Optional Carroburg Crimson wash on the pink areas
8. White dot eyes for that beady look

The UHU glue spit effect: just dab it on with a toothpick. Done.

This method works great for Age of Sigmar orruks and any grimdark greenskin army. Airbrush the Pallid Witch Flesh for even faster results. The Streaking Grime does most of the work—these orcs came out way greener than expected while keeping that sickly pale undertone.

videos.title: Preshading 90 Goblins
videos.description:
Airbrush preshading 90 goblins with Hex Lichen and ink zenithal technique! Batch painting prep for an entire orcs and goblins army in one session.

Mass airbrush preshading session for 90 goblins using the Ninjon-style unified color approach. The goal: create consistent volumes across the entire army before any detail painting begins, keeping everything looking like it belongs together despite varied color schemes.

The technique: Hex Lichen purple from below (shadows), white/ink mix from above (zenithal highlights). This creates pre-built contrast that shows through subsequent layers. When you blast on your actual colors, all the shading is already done.

Workspace tip: clear everything from the spray area before airbrushing. Overspray happens, and you don't want it on finished models. Sort as you go—robed goblins in one area (more airbrush work possible), bare-skin goblins in another.

The squigs got the same treatment and looked incredible after one pass of magenta over the preshading. All volumes defined, ready for detail work. That's the power of proper preshading.

Lesson learned the hard way: don't rush between coats. Started the green goblin skin before the ink dried and got a mottled effect. Called it for the day rather than making it worse. Sleep deprivation plus airbrushing equals problems.

videos.title: How to glaze for fun and profit.
videos.description:
Late night glazing tutorial on Mangler Squig skin! How to build up smooth color transitions using thin layers of paint, taking your dry brush or airbrush foundation to the next level.

Glazing is all about thin paint and knowing your colors. Using Scale Color Primary Red, orange, and yellow-orange ink, I show how to build up highlights from the dry brush foundation we established in the previous video.

Key technique: always pull paint from shadow to highlight—this deposits more pigment where you want the color brightest. Start with thinned primary red (thinner than base coat but not quite wash-thin), hit all raised areas. Mix in orange progressively, becoming more selective about where you apply each layer.

The secret nobody tells you: you need to know your paint's consistency. This is why constantly switching paint brands hurts your progress—you never learn how much water to add. Stick with one brand and learn it. Craft paints don't work for glazing because the pigment breaks up when thinned.

For the top squig, I started one step brighter (orange mixed in from the start) to create the illusion he's catching more light than the bottom squig in shadow. About 35-40 minutes of glazing transforms both models from dry brushed foundation to box art quality skin. You could go longer for more contrast, but this is army-painting efficient.

videos.title: How to Paint Red - Airbrush vs Drybrush
videos.description:
How to paint red squig skin—airbrush vs drybrush comparison! Two methods to achieve the same Mangler Squig box art style, proving you don't need an airbrush.

Starting from a black prime with rattle can white from above, I demonstrate both techniques side by side. The key to vibrant red over dark primer: purple from below (Hex Lyken), then burnt umber to warm the midtones, THEN red (Scale Color Primary Red). Going straight red over black gives muddy maroon.

Airbrush method: spray purple from below, burnt umber across, then red from above. Magenta ink re-establishes purple shadows if you overdo the red. Finish with tangerine ink highlights on top.

Drybrush method: same colors, same order, just with a soft-bristled dry brush. Work purple into shadows, burnt umber over white areas overlapping into purple, then red covering everything. The soft bristles prevent brush strokes.

The magic unifier: a violet oil wash (cheap Walmart paint + mineral spirits) dabbed on and stippled off with paper towel. This ties together any spots you couldn't reach with the dry brush and unifies the whole color scheme. Final result: both methods look nearly identical at 66 minutes for two squigs.

videos.title: How to prepare bases for an entire army quickly and cheaply.
videos.description:
How to base 250+ miniatures quickly and cheaply using homemade basing material! The palette knife technique that turns tedious work into a fast assembly line.

Sometimes the most productive hobby days aren't glamorous. In two hours, I based all the orcs and goblins using that DIY basing mixture I made earlier—and only used half the jar.

The technique: scoop basing material with a palette knife, apply firmly but gently to the base. Don't push all the way down—let the natural texture come through without obvious knife strokes. Keep a bucket of water nearby to quickly rinse your fingers between bases.

Pro tip: use your wet finger to clean the base rim instead of paper towels. Way faster and no waste. Just wipe, rinse, next base.

The basing material recipe (from a previous video): dried coffee grounds baked in the oven, two parts material, two parts spackle, one part paint. Mix and store in a jar—lasts forever and costs almost nothing.

videos.title: What 20 Days of Daily Vlogging Taught Me About Creativity
videos.description:
How do you actually create space for creativity? I break down the mental, physical, and financial aspects of making time to create art every day—lessons learned from 20 days of daily vlogging.

The biggest mindset shift came from Steven Presfield's "The War of Art": creativity isn't about motivation or inspiration, it's about showing up every day. You create the space—mentally, physically, financially—and the creativity shows up.

For mental space, I use rituals: lighting a candle or olive oil lamp, then 30 seconds with a singing bowl to clear my head. That repetitive focus becomes my "clarion call to the muse." Your physical space doesn't need to be fancy—mine was once just a large sketchbook, cutting mat, and wet palette while holding a baby on the couch.

The financial lesson took years: stop trying to make money FROM art. Instead, figure out how to make enough money in as little time as possible so you can keep your creative work pure and not burn out. Art became joyful again when I stopped thinking about what would sell.

I also talk about the journey from being inspired to being inspiring—moving from emulating others to creating something genuinely new. Plus, these Spellcrow orcs I'm building are incredible multipart sculpts for my Old World army project.

videos.title: My 12-Year-Old Airbrush Died Mid-Priming
videos.description:
Airbrush died mid-army prime but the show goes on! Hack for priming without a working airbrush needle, plus first live stream announcement.

RIP to my 12-year-old airbrush—it finally gave up while priming an entire Circles Orboros army. After two weeks of troubleshooting, it's time to accept defeat. But the project couldn't wait, so improvisation happened.

The hack: pull the needle completely out, back it all the way, and the airbrush becomes a spray gun with no trigger control. Press the button and it blasts. Janky? Yes. Effective? Also yes. Got everything primed black before the airbrush totally died.

The last model got brush primed because even the hack stopped working by the end. But the army is ready for zenithal tomorrow—probably rattle can since the airbrush is done.

This changes the workflow significantly. Usually airbrush does heavy lifting on army painting. Now it's all brush skills, knocking the dust off techniques that haven't been primary tools in a while.

Big announcement: first live stream happening Wednesday at 1pm Eastern. New streaming setup finally ready. Come hang out while I ramble and paint—it's going to be fun.

videos.title: How to Strip Metal Models | Call to Arms Vlog Prep Day 1
videos.description:
How to strip metal miniatures with acetone! Day 1 of Call to Arms tournament prep—stripping eBay rescues and getting an army ready in 3 weeks.

For metal models only (not plastic or resin!), 100% acetone is the fastest stripping method. Put models in a metal cup, cover, and wait a few hours. Pop models off bases first—acetone will melt plastic. Roughing up the paint helps the acetone get underneath.

Use a hard bristled brush on cardboard to scrub off softened paint. It makes a mess but works incredibly fast. Filter the acetone through a cone filter to remove debris and reuse it. Rinse with water between scrubbing sessions.

The challenge: need the army playable while painting it for the next two weeks. Can't just strip everything and wait—need to base, prime, and maintain a functional army for practice games. Chess clock practice and physical model movement are as important as the painting.

Discovered some new-in-box models I forgot about, so adjusting the plan on the fly. Some eBay models are already painted well enough to just touch up rather than strip. Day 1 is all sorting, stripping, cleaning mold lines, and getting everything organized for the two-week sprint.

videos.title: How to Use Dirty Down Rust + Creating Blood & Gore - Part 2
videos.description:
Dirty Down Rust tutorial plus creating blood and gore effects with UHU glue! Finishing the Trench Crusade Anchorite Shrine with weathering and disgusting details.

After sealing with matte varnish, apply Dirty Down Rust to metal areas, then hit with a heat gun to activate the bubbling texture. Use water to reactivate dried rust and stipple for more organic effects. Wipe off high points with a damp brush to reveal silver underneath—rust sits in recesses naturally.

Final step: dry brush Mithril Silver on chain links and highest points to show wear through the rust.

Then the blood and gore. Blood for the Blood God technical paint goes on first for the glossy blood effect. The secret sauce: UHU glue mixed with Blood Angels Contrast paint creates stringy, disgusting gore. Apply with brush, then use a gloved finger to pull and stretch the glue into thin strands across the torture wheel.

It's ridiculous and over-the-top—perfect for Trench Crusade. I may have taken it slightly too far for my personal taste, but that's the aesthetic. The gore effect covers up areas where we didn't spend much time painting and adds incredible visual impact for minimal effort.

videos.title: How to Paint the Anchorite Shrine
videos.description:
How to paint the Trench Crusade Anchorite Shrine with Catherine Wheel! Speed painting a centerpiece model using dry brush, contrast paints, and Streaking Grime.

The Anchorite Shrine is mostly silver with a torture wheel and prisoner—perfect for the grim dark treatment. Start with purple dry brush in all the shadows to add depth, then dry brush Mithril Silver over everything. The purple ties this model into the rest of the warband since the silver speed paint we used has purple undertones.

For the Catherine Wheel: Gilman Flesh mixed with Plaguebearer Flesh creates aged, weathered wood over the zenithal. Slap it on sloppy—this is a torture device, not fine furniture. Blood Angel's Red contrast for the hood ties it to the warband's color scheme.

The prisoner on the wheel gets sketched highlights with white primer, then Streaking Grime unifies everything and creates natural shadows with minimal effort. Just slather, wait, and wipe off with makeup sponge.

Total time for the whole Trench Crusade warband including this shrine: about 3-4 hours. Tomorrow I'll add bases, edge highlights, and rust effects to really finish everything off. The warband already looks cohesive and tabletop-ready thanks to the consistent purple shadows and red accent scheme.

videos.title: How to Paint Trench Pilgrims Fast
videos.description:
How to paint Trench Crusade Ecclesiastical Prisoners in 15-20 minutes! Side-by-side comparison of oil wash vs Streaking Grime methods for fast grimdark results.

Painting two prisoners simultaneously—one with oils, one with acrylics and Streaking Grime—to show you both options work great. Starting materials: Battleship Gray speed paint for cloth, Flesh Wash mixed with Bony Matter for skin.

Oil method: Modified Zorn palette (burnt umber, black, yellow ochre, red, white) for everything. Slather brown-black mix on the robes, wipe down to shadows with makeup sponge. Mix flesh tones for skin highlights, blend with a filbert brush. Add gray mixed from white, black, and blue for robe highlights.

Streaking Grime method: Just slather it on, wait, wipe off with makeup sponge going left to right (not up and down—might break the mini). Done.

Pro tip: leave more brown in the shadows at the bottom of robes. I went too far with gray highlights and lost contrast—Streaking Grime actually fixes this by adding grime back into shadows.

Final touch: dry brush metallic silver on helmets and chains. These are throwaway units that exist to die on objectives—don't spend hours on them. 15-minute tabletop ready grimdark!

#trenchcrusade #speedpaint

videos.title: How to Paint Trench Crusade Communicant
videos.description:
How to paint Trench Crusade Communicant in grim dark style using oil paints! Fast technique for undead skin and weathered metal that looks amazing on tabletop.

This Communicant is essentially three colors: silver armor, bronze/gold accents, and bluish undead skin. Perfect candidate for the oil wash method that makes grim dark painting so fast.

Metal: Army Painter Broadsword Silver speed paint, then Halfork Gold for accents. Let dry, then slather on an oil wash (burnt umber, black, yellow ochre mixed with mineral spirits). Wipe off with makeup sponge—instant weathered metal.

Skin: Same modified Zorn palette—blue mixed into the brown wash for that undead coldness matching the official art. Slather it on, wait 10 minutes, remove most with makeup sponge. Then highlight with mostly white plus a touch of blue, dotting on the raised muscles. Blend with a filbert brush.

The whole model could be done in 20 minutes of actual paint time. Oils are magic for this style—forgiving, blendable, and the results look incredible. If you wanted warm skin instead, you could base coat in Rakarth Flesh and just use Streaking Grime, but the blue wash matches the creepy official aesthetic perfectly.

#trenchcrusade #grimdark

videos.title: Fixing Warped Terrain Boards
videos.description:
How to fix warped terrain boards and salvage your modular gaming table project! Gluing foam tiles to MDF backing and planning a 4x4 wargaming surface.

Despite using a drill press to weigh them down, most of my foam terrain tiles warped—corners higher than centers, creating deep seams. One tile stayed flat (the mystery of crafting), but the rest needed fixing. Solution: mount them on MDF backing with Eco Weld adhesive and weigh down with books overnight.

The plan for my modular board: glue four tiles permanently to MDF for a solid 2x2 center, keep the hill tile removable for flexibility, and create transitionary tiles where the perfect stone squares crumble into natural earth. I'll use leftover foam offcuts as scattered broken tiles for extra character.

This project taught me patience—the block boards needed 36+ hours to dry from all the glue between layers. Sometimes you just have to wait. Next up: finishing the terrain board so I can move on to commissions, my Call to Arms army, and Trench Crusade models. When you're juggling multiple projects, sometimes the boring fix-it days are what keep everything moving forward.

#terrainbuilding #wargaming

videos.title: Expanding our tile into a full game board.
videos.description:
Expanding a single terrain tile into a full modular game board! Taking the prototype terrain piece and building out additional tiles to create a complete wargaming table.

After testing the concept with a single tile, it's time to scale up. I'm cutting matching foam tiles and working out the system for connecting them seamlessly. The goal is a modular board where individual tiles lock together but can be rearranged for different battlefield layouts.

This video covers the planning phase: figuring out tile dimensions, how edges will connect, and what terrain features to include. I want variety across the tiles—some open areas, some with rocky outcrops, maybe a hill tile—while keeping a consistent visual style throughout.

Building modular terrain is a bigger upfront investment than a single gaming mat, but the payoff is huge. You get a three-dimensional table with real terrain features, plus the flexibility to change your setup between games. I share my thought process on balancing ambition with actually finishing the project. The key is committing to a manageable scope rather than planning an infinite modular system you'll never complete.

videos.title: A Game Board Turned Into Its Own World
videos.description:
Wargaming terrain board expansion plans and reconnecting with the hobby community! Casual vlog about terrain building ideas and feeling part of the miniature painting world again.

Sometimes the best hobby sessions are just thinking out loud about where projects are heading. Today's rambling covers terrain board expansion—how to grow a gaming table from a small skirmish setup into something that can handle full-scale battles.

The key insight: modular terrain is everything. Building individual pieces that can be rearranged means your terrain collection grows with your gaming needs. Start with the basics (hills, scatter terrain, line-of-sight blockers) and expand from there. Each piece should work with multiple game systems.

Also talking about something less tangible but equally important—feeling connected to the hobby community again. Sharing work online, watching other creators, participating in discussions... these social elements fuel the hobby in ways that solo painting sessions can't replicate. We paint alone but we're part of something bigger.

Whether you're planning your first terrain board or expanding an existing collection, the approach is the same: one piece at a time, keeping modularity in mind. The goal is a gaming table that inspires stories, not just a surface to roll dice on.

videos.title: Dirty Down Moss Tutorial (Heat Gun Technique)
videos.description:
Dirty Down Moss tutorial with heat gun technique! Watch moss effects bubble and expand in real time on this terrain board build—plus disaster recovery when things go wrong.

Day seven of the terrain build and it's moss time. The secret to getting Dirty Down Moss to really pop: hit it with a heat gun while it's still wet. The heat makes it bubble and expand, creating incredible organic texture. Credit to Trent's video for the scientific approach—I'm just winging it.

Fair warning: I accidentally switched my heat gun to maximum and melted several foam squares. The fix? AK Interactive paste to fill the damaged areas, then reactivating the moss with water and blending it in. Actually created a cool natural effect I want to replicate on the whole board.

Also covered: making grass tufts from long static grass (pinch in the middle, glue, instant double tuft), adding skulls from the bits box for character, and knowing when to stop before you moss everything in sight. This board went way more mossy than intended, but sometimes you just have to embrace where the project takes you.

videos.title: How to turn foam to stone.
videos.description:
How to turn foam into convincing stone texture! Filling those visible foam cells and creating rock-like terrain using AK Interactive texture paste.

Day 5 of the terrain build. Woke up ready to paint, then noticed the foam cells still visible on the tall stones—that spongy texture ruins the stone illusion. Quick fix before painting: AK Interactive Natural Texture Earth paste applied with a palette knife to fill in those pores.

The technique: dip your spatula, dab it onto the foam sides, let it create natural texture. Yes, it's extra work, but every time you use this board, you'd notice those unfilled cells and regret being lazy. Better to do it right now.

Also covered: adding cardstock reinforcement to thin pieces for rigidity, and basing the 20mm miniatures with the same texture paste. The paste creates a slight cracking effect when applied thin—perfect for natural earth look. Go thicker or double-layer if you want solid coverage.

By end of day: all supports removed from prints, bases done, terrain sealed with gray primer, and card sleeves made. The foam officially looks like stone now, not craft store styrofoam. Tomorrow we paint!

videos.title: Magnetizing Bases and Fixing a Foam Board Disaster
videos.description:
Day 4 of the hobby vlog! Magnetizing bases, terrain board troubleshooting, and getting more comfortable talking to a camera. Quick tip: put magnets on printer paper when gluing—catches the white residue from superglue.

Got back from playing Trench Crusade at a store near the beach (footage included!). Hung out with the homies and had a great time. Also maybe printed and framed some YouTube comments as a thank you... hopefully that's not too creepy for a 35-year-old man to do.

The Molly Crew terrain board hit a snag: the porous mystery foam didn't bond between blocks like I hoped. The glue just soaked into each piece instead of creating a solid connection. If you're doing this yourself, use XPS or pink insulation foam instead—we know how that behaves.

My fix: blasted it with latex-based spray primer (4-5 coats) before leaving, and I'll seal it with thinned Modge Podge to bind everything together while keeping the natural stone texture. Planning to build a shadow box frame around the 10x10 board to lock it all in place. Might grab a thick picture frame from the thrift store for a cool border.

Weekends are rough for hobby time, but weekdays give me more space to dig into projects. Day 4 and already feeling more natural on camera!

videos.title: 2 Color NMM Easy Peasy Style
videos.description:
Learn two-color non-metallic metal (NMM) gold that anyone can do! In this tutorial, I paint a Peradeth miniature using just yellow ochre and yellow from Scale 75, proving that NMM doesn't have to be intimidating.

I walk through my entire process: cleaning mold lines with a half-round file, basing with super glue (using water as an accelerator!), rattle can priming with a zenithal highlight, and then the actual NMM technique. The secret? Very thin paint—almost glaze consistency—and building up layers gradually. I show exactly how thin my paint is on my hand so you can replicate it.

The technique is basically controlled stippling and edge highlighting with watered-down paint. Don't be scared of NMM—we're just poking the miniature with watery paint! I add a third color (violet) as a very thin glaze in the shadows to add depth and tie the gold into the rest of the model.

I also keep it real about painting with a busted brush (paint in the ferrule, tip going three directions) and demonstrate that you can still get great results with imperfect tools. The key is using quality paints—Scale 75 Artist Acrylics do a lot of heavy lifting here.

This is the first video in my daily hobby vlog series, and I share some honest thoughts about pushing through obstacles to actually start creating.

#nmm #miniaturepainting
